evidence: README.md defines familycatalog as the contract declaration entrypoint, but upload contract governance is re-asserted in multiple test files with local logic and hard-coded expectations.

goseg/uploadsvc/contractspec/contractspec_test.go hard-codes the action cardinality (`len(availableActions) != 2`) and explicit checks for only `open-endpoint` and `reset`, creating a compatibility contract outside the catalog boundary.

goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go reconstructs canonical mappings by iterating over `contracts.ActionContractBindings()` while also depending on conformance fixtures, duplicating governance checks already covered by protocol conformance tests.

goseg/protocol/contracts/familycatalog/catalog.go splits action specs across multiple constructors and recombines in `AllActionSpecs`, while downstream checks in uploadsvc do not consume a single authoritative compatibility policy API.

findings.summary: Upload contract behavior is validated through protocol-internal registries directly from uploadsvc tests, creating a wide cross-package coupling surface.
evidence: TestUploadParserActionsMatchContractFixtures in goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go builds expectations from conformance.ActionContractFixtures() and contracts.ActionContractBindings(), tying uploadsvc tests to protocol registry internals.

The same action identifiers/contracts are authored in familycatalog/catalog.go, but uploadsvc tests re-derive expectations by scanning protocol-level bindings rather than consuming a dedicated upload contract API.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a narrow protocol-facing upload contract accessor (single source for upload namespace bindings) and have uploadsvc tests depend on that API only; keep conformance fixture validation inside protocol/contracts tests.

findings.summary: Contract invariants are duplicated as local literals/count assumptions in uploadsvc tests instead of being enforced through one architectural authority.
evidence: goseg/uploadsvc/contractspec/contractspec_test.go asserts len(availableActions) == 2 and separately checks explicit open-endpoint/reset presence.
goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go independently resolves expected contract IDs by iterating contracts.ActionContractBindings().
goseg/protocol/contracts/familycatalog/catalog.go already defines upload action specs/contract IDs, so behavior is validated via repeated patterns across files rather than one boundary contract.
findings.suggestion: Replace hard-coded cardinality/name checks with table-driven assertions sourced from a single exported upload contract descriptor list; keep only behavior-specific assertions in uploadsvc tests.

findings.summary: Contract compatibility policy is fragmented across README and multiple test-local assertions.
evidence: README.md declares governance entrypoints and quality-gate ownership, but enforcement details are duplicated in uploadsvc tests rather than exposed as one policy surface.
contractspec_test encodes compatibility via `expected exactly two supported upload actions` and hard-coded action presence checks.
actions_test independently recomputes expected canonical bindings per action, adding another policy copy path.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single exported compatibility-policy API in the contracts layer (for required upload actions and expected bindings) and make both uploadsvc test suites consume that API instead of hard-coded counts/action lists or bespoke binding scans.

findings.summary: Upload service contract tests depend directly on conformance fixture representations.
evidence: uploadsvc/actions_test.go imports `protocol/contracts/conformance` and builds `fixtureByAction` from `ActionContractFixtures()` instead of consuming a stable contract registry surface.
conformance/fixtures_test.go validates fixture correctness within the contract subsystem, so adapter-layer tests also pulling fixture internals creates boundary overlap.
uploadsvc/doc.go describes strict contract validation behavior at package boundary, but tests bind to internal fixture modeling rather than a consumer-focused contract API.
findings.suggestion: Keep fixture-shape validation inside the conformance package, and expose a stable consumer-facing contract lookup from the contracts package for uploadsvc tests. Refactor uploadsvc tests to assert against that API so conformance fixture representation can evolve without adapter-layer churn.
